revision.text: The Season of the Going Out could be considered to be the [[History of Egypt|Ancient Egyptian]] [[Summer]] and during this period the farmers would plow their fields. The term, "Going Out" refers to the receding of the Nile floodwaters. This season was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] and occurred after the [[Season of the Inundation]]

The '''Season of the Going Out''' could be considered to be the [[History of Egypt|Ancient Egyptian]] [[Summer]] and during this period the farmers would plow their fields. The term, "Going Out" refers to the receding of the [[Nile]] floodwaters. This season was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] and occurred after the [[Season of the Inundation]]

The '''Season of the Going Out''' and is the ancient [[History of Egypt| Egyptian]] [[Summer]] and during this period the farmers would plow their fields. The term, "Going Out" refers to the receding of the [[Nile]] floodwaters. This season was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] and occurred after the [[Season of the Inundation]]

The '''Season of the Going Out''' is the ancient [[History of Egypt| Egyptian]] [[Summer]] and during this period the farmers would plow their fields. The term, "Going Out" refers to the receding of the [[Nile]] floodwaters. This season was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] and occurred after the [[Season of the Inundation]]

The '''Season of the Going Out''' is the ancient [[History of Egypt|Egyptian]] [[summer]] and during this period the farmers would plow their fields. The term, "Going Out" refers to the receding of the [[Nile]] floodwaters. This season was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] and occurred after the [[Season of the Inundation]]
{{stub}}

The '''Season of the Emergence''' ([[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''prt'', <hiero>pr*r:t*hrw</hiero>) is the second season in the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]], following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptian peasants to plow and plant their fields.
The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of [[November]] to the middle of [[March]]. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi.
It was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].

The '''Season of the Emergence''' ([[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''prt'', <hiero>pr*r:t*hrw</hiero>) is the second season in the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]], following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptian peasants to plow and plant their fields.
The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of [[November]] to the middle of [[March]]. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi.
It was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].
[[category:ancient Egypt]]

It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptian peasants to plow and plant their fields. The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi. It was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]]. [[Category:Ancient Egyptian society]]

{{Hiero|[[Per (hieroglyph)|per]]--t — Season
of the Emergence |pr:r:V12-N5-.- or -.-O1:,:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ([[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''prt'') is the second season in the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]] (running from "roughly mid-November to mid-March."David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93), following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptians to plow and plant their fields.[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi. It was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]]. ==References==
[[Category:Ancient Egyptian society]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}}

{{Hiero|[[Per (hieroglyph)|per]]--t — Season
of the Emergence |pr:r:V12-N5-.- or -.-O1:,:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ([[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''prt''), often written as '''Peret''' Nigel C. Strudwick, ''Texts from the Pyramid Age'', SBL 2005, p.103, '''Pert''' etc, is the second season in the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]] (running from "roughly mid-November to mid-March."David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93), following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptians to plow and plant their fields.[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi. It was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]]. ==References==
[[Category:Ancient Egyptian society]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}}

Strudwick, ''Texts from the Pyramid Age'', SBL 2005, p.103, '''Pert''' etc, is the second season in the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]] (running from "roughly mid-November to mid-March."David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93), following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptians to plow and plant their fields.[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi. It was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] or Shemu. ==References==
[[Category:Ancient Egyptian society]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}}

Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi. It is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] or Shemu. ==References==

{{Hiero|[[Per (hieroglyph)|per]]--t — Season
of the Emergence |pr:r:V12-N5-.- or -.-O1:,:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ([[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''prt''), or '''Peret''' Nigel C. Strudwick, ''Texts from the Pyramid Age'', SBL 2005, p.103, etc, was the second or [[Winter]] season of the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]] (running from "roughly mid-November to mid-March."David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93), following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptians to plant their fields.[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi. It is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] or Shemu. ==References==
[[Category:Ancient Egyptian calendar]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}} [[fr:Peret]] [[pl:Peret]]

{{Hiero|[[Per (hieroglyph)|per]]--t — Season
of the Emergence |pr:r:V12-N5-.- or -.-O1:,:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ([[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''prt''), or '''Peret''' Nigel C. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93), following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptians to plant their fields.[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi. BK BK BK BK BK BK BK BK BK BK BK BK BK BK RULEZ It is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] or Shemu. ==References==

Strudwick, ''Texts from the Pyramid Age'', SBL 2005, p.103, etc, was the second or [[Winter]] season of the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]] (running from "roughly mid-November to mid-March."David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93), following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptians to plant their fields.[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University]because of the nile river it allowed them to get their lands to be fertile and let them have the season of emergence. The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Tybi, Mechir, Phamenoth, and Pharmuthi. The foundation of this was from BK. It is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]] or Shemu. ==References==

{{Hiero|[[Per (hieroglyph)|per]]--t — Season
of the Emergence |pr:r:V12-N5-.- or -.-O1:,:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ([[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''prt''), or '''Peret''' Nigel C. Strudwick, ''Texts from the Pyramid Age'', SBL 2005, p.103, etc, was the second or [[Winter]] season of the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]] (running from "roughly mid-November to mid-March."David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93), following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptians to plant their fields.[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University]because of the nile river it allowed them to get their lands to be fertile and let them have the season of emergence. The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Inundation consists of four 30-day months. These months can be either referred to by number (months 5 through 8) or by names as follows: * Month 5 = [[Month of Tobi|Tobi]] * Month 6 = [[Meshir]] * Month 7 = [[Paremhat]] * Month 8 = [[Paremoude]] The Season of Inundation is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==References==
[[Category:Ancient Egyptian calendar]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}} [[fr:Peret]] [[pl:Peret]]

Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Emergence consists of four 30-day months. {{Hiero|[[Per (hieroglyph)|per]]--t — Season

Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93), following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. It received its name because this was the period when the [[Nile]] flood waters receded, and allowed the Egyptians to plant their fields.[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University]because of the nile river it allowed them to get their lands to be fertile and let them have the season of emergence. Also, the sun ate their eyes out and burned the fields of grass during this year. They were utterly extinctified by the next season. The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Emergence consists of four 30-day months. These months can be either referred to by number (months 5 through 8) or by names as follows: * Month 5 = [[Month of Tobi|Tobi]] * Month 6 = [[Meshir]] * Month 7 = [[Paremhat]] * Month 8 = [[Paremoude]] The Season of Inundation is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==References==

{{Hiero|[[Per (hieroglyph)|per]]--t — Season

Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93), following the [[Season of the Inundation]]. Peret was the season where the annual Nile flood retreated leaving a rich and fertile layer of black silt over the land ideal for planting crops. The Egyptian growing season consisted of the 120 days of Peret where planting and growth took place and was followed by the 120 days of the Summer season where the harvest took place. This left 120 days for the annual flood which meant for Egyptain farmers there was a 120 day period where work could be focused on monument building. The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Emergence consists of four 30-day months. These months can be either referred to by number (months 5 through 8) or by names as follows: * Month 5 = [[Month of Tobi|Tobi]] * Month 6 = [[Meshir]] * Month 7 = [[Paremhat]] * Month 8 = [[Paremoude]] The Season of Inundation is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==References==

Peret was the season where the annual Nile flood retreated leaving a rich and fertile layer of black silt, made as the floods picked up black soil and rocks, over the land making it ideal for planting crops. The Egyptian growing season consisted of the 120 days of Peret where planting and growth took place and was followed by the 120 days of the Summer season where the harvest took place. This left 120 days for the annual flood which meant for Egyptain farmers there was a 120 day period where work could be focused on monument building. {{Hiero|[[Per (hieroglyph)|per]]--t — Season
of the Emergence |pr:r:V12-N5-.- or -.-O1:,:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ([[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''prt''), or '''Proyet''', or '''Peret''' Nigel C. Strudwick, ''Texts from the Pyramid Age'', SBL 2005, p.103, was the second or [[Winter]] season of the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]] (running from "roughly mid-November to mid-March." David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93). Allthough nowadays the second season, as followed by the Copts and the Ethiopians, begins January 9 (or 10th in leapyears), comprising the months of [[Tobi|Tubah]], [[Meshir]], [[Paremhat]] and [[Paremoude]]. Proyet is following the [[Season of the Inundation]], Akhet. Peret was the season where the annual Nile flood retreated leaving a rich and fertile layer of black silt, made as the floods picked up black soil and rocks, over the land making it ideal for planting crops. The Egyptian growing season consisted of the 120 days of Peret where planting and growth took place and was followed by the 120 days of the Summer season where the harvest took place. This left 120 days for the annual flood which meant for Egyptain farmers there was a 120 day period where work could be focused on monument building. The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Emergence consists of four 30-day months. These months can be either referred to by number (months 5 through 8) or by names as follows: * Month 5 = [[Month of Tobi|Tobi]] * Month 6 = [[Meshir]] * Month 7 = [[Paremhat]] * Month 8 = [[Paremoude]] The Season of Emergence is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==References==
{{start box}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Inundation]]
''Akhet''| title=''[[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian Seasons]]''
[[Season of the Emergence]]
'''''Proyet'''''| years=days: 120 days| after=[[Season of the Harvest]]
''Shemu''}} {{end box}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian calendar]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}} [[fr:Peret]] [[pl:Peret]]

{{Hiero|[[Per (hieroglyph)|per]]--t — Season
of the Emergence |pr:r:V12-N5-.- or -.-O1:,:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ([[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''prt''), or '''Proyet''', or '''Peret''' Nigel C. Strudwick, ''Texts from the Pyramid Age'', SBL 2005, p.103, was the second or [[Winter]] season of the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]] (running from '''"roughly mid-November to mid-March."''' David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93). Allthough nowadays the second season, as followed by the Copts and the Ethiopians, begins January 9 (or 10th in leapyears), comprising the months of [[Tobi|Tubah]], [[Meshir]], [[Paremhat]] and [[Paremoude]]. Proyet is following the [[Season of the Inundation]], Akhet. Peret was the season where the annual Nile flood retreated leaving a rich and fertile layer of black silt, made as the floods picked up black soil and rocks, over the land making it ideal for planting crops. The Egyptian growing season consisted of the 120 days of Peret where planting and growth took place and was followed by the 120 days of the Summer season where the harvest took place. This left 120 days for the annual flood which meant for Egyptain farmers there was a 120 day period where work could be focused on monument building. The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of November to the middle of March. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Emergence consists of four 30-day months. These months can be either referred to by number (months 5 through 8) or by names as follows: * Month 5 = [[Month of Tobi|Tobi]] * Month 6 = [[Meshir]] * Month 7 = [[Paremhat]] * Month 8 = [[Paremoude]] The Season of Emergence is preceded by the [[Season of the Inundation]] and is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==References==
{{start box}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Inundation]]
''Akhet''| title=''[[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian Seasons]]''
[[Season of the Emergence]]
'''''Proyet'''''| years=days: 120 days| after=[[Season of the Harvest]]
''Shemu''}} {{end box}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian calendar]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}} [[fr:Peret]] [[pl:Peret]]

These months can be either referred to by number (months 5 through 8) or by names as follows: * Month 5 = [[Month of Tobi|Tobi]] * Month 6 = [[Meshir]] * Month 7 = [[Paremhat]] * Month 8 = [[Paremoude]] The Season of Emergence is preceded by the [[Season of the Inundation]] and is followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 י ==See also== *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Coptic calendar]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{start box}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Inundation]]

Strudwick, ''Texts from the Pyramid Age'', SBL 2005, p.103 was the second season or [[Winter]] season of the [[Egyptian calendar]]. It fell roughly between early January and early May.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 Peret falls after the [[Season of the Inundation]], also known as '''Akhet'''. Peret was the season where the annual Nile flood retreated leaving a rich and fertile layer of black silt, made as the floods picked up black soil and rocks, over the land making it ideal for planting crops. The Egyptian growing season lasted for 5 months, where planting and growth took place. It was followed by the 120 days of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. The Season of the Emergence is known as '''Peret''' in both the lunar and the civil Egyptian calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from early January to early May. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Emergence consists of four 30-day months. These months can be either referred to by number (months 5 through 8) or by names as follows: * Month 5 = [[Month of Tobi|Sf-bdt]] * Month 6 = [[Meshir|Rekh wer]] * Month 7 = [[Paremhat|Rekh neds]] * Month 8 = [[Paremoude|Renwet]] The Season of Emergence was preceded by the [[Season of the Inundation]] and was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0-87169-214-7, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Inundation]]

The Egyptian growing season lasted for 5 months, where planting and growth took place. It was followed by the 120 days of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. The Season of the Emergence is known as '''Peret''' in both the lunar and the civil Egyptian calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from early January to early May. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Emergence consists of four 30-day months. These months can be either referred to by number (months 5 through 8) or by names as follows: * Month 5 = [[Month of Tobi|Sf-bdt]] * Month 6 = [[Meshir|Rekh wer]] * Month 7 = [[Paremhat|Rekh neds]] * Month 8 = [[Paremoude|Renwet]] ... The Season of Emergence was preceded by the [[Season of the Inundation]] and was followed by the [[Season of the Harvest]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0-87169-214-7, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Inundation]]

Season of the Emergence
'''''Peret'''''| years=days: 120 days| after=[[Season of the Harvest]]
''Shemu''}} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Emergence}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] {{AncientEgypt-stub}}

{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Emergence{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Emergence include O1:D21-X1:N5, O1-X1, O1-X1:N5, O1-Z5 and O1-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and pr:r:V12-N5 and O1:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t.{{fact}}}}}}
Prt|O1:D21|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:prt|Prt]]}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[Transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|[[Transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Šmw]]}}'').{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book |date=1989 |isbn=0-87169-214-7 |p=5 }}. {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |p=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{fact}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and to the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |p=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided between four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decan]]s. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

(''[[wikt:prt|Prt]]'')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:prt|Prt]]}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[Transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}''). {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |p=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{fact|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and to the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |p=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decan]]s. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

(''[[wikt:prt|Prt]]'')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:prt|Prt]]}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[Transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}''). {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and to the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

(''[[wikt:prt|Prt]]'')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:prt|Prt]]}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[Transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}''). In the modern Coptic calendar, the season falls between [[Paopi 10]] and [[Tobi 10]].{{Cite book |url=https://www.loc.gov/resource/ihas.200155823.0/?sp=8 |title=The Divine Liturgies of Saints Basil, Gregory, and Cyril |publisher=St. Mary & St. George Coptic Orthodox Church, Coptic Orthodox Diocese of the Southern United States |year=2001 |location=Tallahassee, Florida, 2001. |pages=171-176}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and to the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. In the modern Coptic calendar, the season falls between [[Paopi 10]] and [[Tobi 10]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! (''[[wikt:prt|Prt]]'')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:prt|Prt]]}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[Transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] (about 9 January), continues through the months of [[Meshir]] and [[Paremhat]], before concluding at the end of [[Parmouti]] (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{harvp|Tetley|2014|p=[http://www.egyptchronology.com/uploads/2/6/9/4/26943741/ch_3_investigating_ancient_egyptian_calendars.pdf 39]}}.{{sfnp|Winlock|1940|p=453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and to the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. In the modern Coptic calendar, the season falls between [[Paopi 10]] and [[Tobi 10]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! revision.comment: /* Months */ erroneous information not actually supported by citation.
revision.text:
{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Emergence{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Emergence include O1:D21, O1-X1, O1-X1:N5, O1-Z5 and O1-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and pr:r:V12-N5 and {{nowrap|O1:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}}}
(''[[wikt:prt|Prt]]'')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:prt|Prt]]}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[Transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] (about 9 January), continues through the months of [[Meshir]] and [[Paremhat]], before concluding at the end of [[Parmouti]] (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{harvp|Tetley|2014|p=[http://www.egyptchronology.com/uploads/2/6/9/4/26943741/ch_3_investigating_ancient_egyptian_calendars.pdf 39]}}.{{sfnp|Winlock|1940|p=453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and to the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''
''Sf Bdt'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] |- | II ''Prt''
''Mḫr'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Meshir]] |- | III ''Prt''
''Rh Nds'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paremhat]] |- | IV ''Prt''
''Rnwt'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paremoude]] |} ==See also== * [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Inundation]]
''Ꜣḫt'' |title=''[[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian Seasons]]''
Season of the Emergence
''Prt'' |years=days: 120 days |after=[[Season of the Harvest]]
''Šmw''}} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Emergence}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]]

(''[[wikt:prt|Prt]]'')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:prt|Prt]]}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[Transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] (about 9 January), continues through the months of [[Meshir]] and [[Paremhat]], before concluding at the end of [[Parmouti]] (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''
''Sf Bdt'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] |- | II ''Prt''
''Mḫr'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Meshir]] |- | III ''Prt''
''Rh Nds'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paremhat]] |- | IV ''Prt''
''Rnwt'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paremoude]] |} ==See also== * [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Inundation]]
''Ꜣḫt'' |title=''[[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian Seasons]]''
Season of the Emergence
''Prt'' |years=days: 120 days |after=[[Season of the Harvest]]
''Šmw''}} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Emergence}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]]

{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Emergence{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Emergence include O1:D21, O1-X1, O1-X1:N5, O1-Z5 and O1-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and pr:r:V12-N5 and {{nowrap|O1:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}}}

It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] (about 9 January), continues through the months of [[Meshir]] and [[Paremhat]], before concluding at the end of [[Parmouti]] (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''
''Sf Bdt'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] |- | II ''Prt''
''Mḫr'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Meshir]] |- | III ''Prt''
''Rh Nds'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paremhat]] |- | IV ''Prt''
''Rnwt'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Emergence}}

''Šmw''}} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Emergence}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]]

Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! (''[[wikt:prt|Prt]]'')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:prt|Prt]]}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[Transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] (about 9 January), continues through the months of [[Meshir]] and [[Paremhat]], before concluding at the end of [[Parmouti]] (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''
''Sf Bdt'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] |- | II ''Prt''
''Mḫr'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Meshir]] |- | III ''Prt''
''Rh Nds'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paremhat]] |- | IV ''Prt''
''Rnwt'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paremoude]] |} ==See also== * [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Inundation]]
''Ꜣḫt'' |title=''[[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian Seasons]]''
Season of the Emergence
''Prt'' |years=days: 120 days |after=[[Season of the Harvest]]
''Šmw''}} {{S-end}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Season of the Emergence]] [[en:Season of the Emergence]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Emergence}}

Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] (about 9 January), continues through the months of [[Meshir]] and [[Paremhat]], before concluding at the end of [[Parmouti]] (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}} The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''. The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[Nile]] from [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''
''Sf Bdt'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Tobi (month)|Tobi]] |- | II ''Prt''
''Mḫr'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Meshir]] |- | III ''Prt''
''Rh Nds'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paremhat]] |- | IV ''Prt''
''Rnwt'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paremoude]] |} ==See also== * [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Inundation]]
