[[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Emergence{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Emergence include O1:D21, O1-X1, O1-X1:N5, O1-Z5 and O1-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and pr:r:V12-N5 and {{nowrap|O1:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t.}}}}}}
('''')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{langx|egy|}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[:en:Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|}}''). In the [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[:en:Tobi (month)|Tobi]] (about 9 January), continues through the months of [[:en:Meshir|Meshir]] and [[:en:Paremhat|Paremhat]], before concluding at the end of [[:en:Parmouti|Parmouti]] (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[:en:Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''. The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[:en:Nile|Nile]] from [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[:en:Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[:en:Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[:en:Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[:en:Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] or [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[:en:Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[:en:Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''

Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[:en:Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] or [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[:en:Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[:en:Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''

[[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Emergence{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Emergence include O1:D21, O1-X1, O1-X1:N5, O1-Z5 and O1-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and pr:r:V12-N5 and {{nowrap|O1:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t.}}}}}}
('''')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{langx|egy|}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Season of the Inundation]] (''{{lang|egy|[[:en:Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Ꜣḫt]]}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|}}''). In the [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[:en:Tobi (month)|Tobi]] (about 9 January), continues through the months of [[:en:Meshir|Meshir]] and [[:en:Paremhat|Paremhat]], before concluding at the end of [[:en:Parmouti|Parmouti]] (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the [[:en:Egyptian language|Ancient Egyptian]] name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''. The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the [[:en:Nile|Nile]] from [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]] and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[:en:Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[:en:Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[:en:Mesori|fourth month]] of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[:en:Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] or [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[:en:Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan="2" | [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[:en:Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''
''Sf Bdt'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[:en:Tobi (month)|Tobi]] |- | II ''Prt''
''Mḫr'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[:en:Meshir|Meshir]] |- | III ''Prt''
''Rh Nds'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[:en:Paremhat|Paremhat]] |- | IV ''Prt''
''Rnwt'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Emergence}}

It fell after the {{ill|Season of the Inundation|en|Season of the Inundation|WD=Q770568|lt=Season of the Inundation}} (''{{lang|egy|{{ill|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|en|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|WD=Q2482781|lt=Ꜣḫt}}}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|}}''). In the {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic}} and {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian}} calendars this season begins at the start of the month of {{ill|Tobi (month)|en|Tobi (month)|WD=Q290480|lt=Tobi}} (about 9 January), continues through the months of {{ill|Meshir|en|Meshir|WD=Q550074|lt=Meshir}} and {{ill|Paremhat|en|Paremhat|WD=Q546893|lt=Paremhat}}, before concluding at the end of {{ill|Parmouti|en|Parmouti|WD=Q2144472|lt=Parmouti}} (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the {{ill|Egyptian language|en|Egyptian language|WD=Q50868|lt=Ancient Egyptian}} name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''. The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the {{ill|Nile|en|Nile|WD=Q3392|lt=Nile}} from {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|WD=Q1519552|lt=its annual flood}} and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[:en:Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[:en:Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the {{ill|Mesori|en|Mesori|WD=Q1621298|lt=fourth month}} of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and {{ill|Roman Egypt|en|Roman Egypt|WD=Q202311|lt=Roman periods}} meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] or {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}}. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. In the {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic}} and {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian}} calendars this season begins at the start of the month of {{ill|Tobi (month)|en|Tobi (month)|WD=Q290480|lt=Tobi}} (about 9 January), continues through the months of {{ill|Meshir|en|Meshir|WD=Q550074|lt=Meshir}} and {{ill|Paremhat|en|Paremhat|WD=Q546893|lt=Paremhat}}, before concluding at the end of {{ill|Parmouti|en|Parmouti|WD=Q2144472|lt=Parmouti}} (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the {{ill|Egyptian language|en|Egyptian language|WD=Q50868|lt=Ancient Egyptian}} name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''. The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the {{ill|Nile|en|Nile|WD=Q3392|lt=Nile}} from {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|WD=Q1519552|lt=its annual flood}} and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[:en:Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[:en:Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the {{ill|Mesori|en|Mesori|WD=Q1621298|lt=fourth month}} of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and {{ill|Roman Egypt|en|Roman Egypt|WD=Q202311|lt=Roman periods}} meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] or {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}}. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[:en:Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic calendar}}. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian}} ! rowspan="2" | {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic}} |- ! {{ill|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|en|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|WD=Q2482781|lt=Transliteration}} ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''
''Sf Bdt'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || {{ill|Tobi (month)|en|Tobi (month)|WD=Q290480|lt=Tobi}} |- | II ''Prt''
''Mḫr'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || {{ill|Meshir|en|Meshir|WD=Q550074|lt=Meshir}} |- | III ''Prt''
''Rh Nds'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || {{ill|Paremhat|en|Paremhat|WD=Q546893|lt=Paremhat}} |- | IV ''Prt''
''Rnwt'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Emergence}}

('''')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{langx|egy|}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian calendar}}s. It fell after the {{ill|Season of the Inundation|en|Season of the Inundation|WD=Q770568|lt=Season of the Inundation}} (''{{lang|egy|{{ill|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|en|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|WD=Q2482781|lt=Ꜣḫt}}}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|}}''). In the {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic}} and {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian}} calendars this season begins at the start of the month of {{ill|Tobi (month)|en|Tobi (month)|WD=Q290480|lt=Tobi}} (about 9 January), continues through the months of {{ill|Meshir|en|Meshir|WD=Q550074|lt=Meshir}} and {{ill|Paremhat|en|Paremhat|WD=Q546893|lt=Paremhat}}, before concluding at the end of {{ill|Parmouti|en|Parmouti|WD=Q2144472|lt=Parmouti}} (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the {{ill|Egyptian language|en|Egyptian language|WD=Q50868|lt=Ancient Egyptian}} name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''. The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the {{ill|Nile|en|Nile|WD=Q3392|lt=Nile}} from {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|WD=Q1519552|lt=its annual flood}} and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and {{ill|Roman Egypt|en|Roman Egypt|WD=Q202311|lt=Roman periods}} meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] or {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}}. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[:en:Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. revision.text:
[[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Emergence{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Emergence include O1:D21, O1-X1, O1-X1:N5, O1-Z5 and O1-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and pr:r:V12-N5 and {{nowrap|O1:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t.}}}}}}
('''')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{langx|egy|}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian calendar}}s. It fell after the {{ill|Season of the Inundation|en|Season of the Inundation|WD=Q770568|lt=Season of the Inundation}} (''{{lang|egy|{{ill|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|en|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|WD=Q2482781|lt=Ꜣḫt}}}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|}}''). In the {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic}} and {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian}} calendars this season begins at the start of the month of {{ill|Tobi (month)|en|Tobi (month)|WD=Q290480|lt=Tobi}} (about 9 January), continues through the months of {{ill|Meshir|en|Meshir|WD=Q550074|lt=Meshir}} and {{ill|Paremhat|en|Paremhat|WD=Q546893|lt=Paremhat}}, before concluding at the end of {{ill|Parmouti|en|Parmouti|WD=Q2144472|lt=Parmouti}} (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the {{ill|Egyptian language|en|Egyptian language|WD=Q50868|lt=Ancient Egyptian}} name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''. The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the {{ill|Nile|en|Nile|WD=Q3392|lt=Nile}} from {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|WD=Q1519552|lt=its annual flood}} and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[:en:Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[:en:Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the {{ill|Mesori|en|Mesori|WD=Q1621298|lt=fourth month}} of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and {{ill|Roman Egypt|en|Roman Egypt|WD=Q202311|lt=Roman periods}} meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] or {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}}. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[:en:Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic calendar}}. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian}} ! rowspan="2" | {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic}} |- ! {{ill|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|en|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|WD=Q2482781|lt=Transliteration}} ! Meaning |- | I ''Prt''
''Sf Bdt'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || {{ill|Tobi (month)|en|Tobi (month)|WD=Q290480|lt=Tobi}} |- | II ''Prt''
''Mḫr'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || {{ill|Meshir|en|Meshir|WD=Q550074|lt=Meshir}} |- | III ''Prt''
''Rh Nds'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || {{ill|Paremhat|en|Paremhat|WD=Q546893|lt=Paremhat}} |- | IV ''Prt''
''Rnwt'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Emergence}}
{{nbsp}} || [[:en:Paremoude|Paremoude]] |} ==See also== * {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian}} & {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic calendar}}s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * {{ill|Temple of Kom Ombo|en|Temple of Kom Ombo|WD=Q1243401|lt=Temple of Kom Ombo}} ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before={{ill|Season of the Inundation|en|Season of the Inundation|WD=Q770568|lt=Season of the Inundation}}
''Ꜣḫt'' |title=''{{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian Seasons}}''
Season of the Emergence
''Prt'' |years=days: 120 days |after=[[Season of the Harvest]]
''Šmw''}} {{S-end}} [[wikt:prt|Prt]] [[wikt:šmw|Šmw]] [[en:Season of the Emergence]] [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]] [[az:Peret]] [[bg:Сезон на покълване]] [[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]] [[es:Estación de la emergencia]] [[fr:Peret]] [[lt:Peretas]] [[ms:Musim Kemunculan]] [[pl:Peret]] [[ro:Peret]] [[ru:Перет]] [[uk:Перет]] [[zh:播種季]] {{wikidata link|Q2037143}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''. The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the {{ill|Nile|en|Nile|WD=Q3392|lt=Nile|1=Nile}} from {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|WD=Q1519552|lt=its annual flood|1=Flooding of the Nile}} and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[:en:Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[:en:Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the {{ill|Mesori|en|Mesori|WD=Q1621298|lt=fourth month|1=Mesori}} of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and {{ill|Roman Egypt|en|Roman Egypt|WD=Q202311|lt=Roman periods|1=Roman Egypt}} meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] or {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}}. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[:en:Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic calendar|1=Coptic calendar}}. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian|1=Egyptian calendar}} ! rowspan="2" | {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic|1=Coptic calendar}} |- ! {{ill|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|en|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|WD=Q2482781|lt=Transliteration|1=Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian}} ! [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] [[ar:برت (فصل مصري)]][[az:Peret]][[bg:Сезон на покълване]][[de:Peret (Altes Ägypten)]][[en:Season of the Emergence]][[es:Estación de la emergencia]][[fr:Peret]][[lt:Peretas]][[ms:Musim Kemunculan]][[pl:Peret]][[ro:Peret]][[ru:Перет]][[uk:Перет]][[zh:播種季]] {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Emergence{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Emergence include O1:D21, O1-X1, O1-X1:N5, O1-Z5 and O1-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and pr:r:V12-N5 and {{nowrap|O1:D21-N31:D54-N36:D54*t.}}}}}}
('''')|O1:D21-X1:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Emergence''' ({{langx|egy|}}) was the second season of the lunar and civil {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian calendar|1=Egyptian calendar}}s. It fell after the {{ill|Season of the Inundation|en|Season of the Inundation|WD=Q770568|lt=Season of the Inundation|1=Season of the Inundation}} (''{{lang|egy|{{ill|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|en|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|WD=Q2482781|lt=Ꜣḫt|1=Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian}}}}'') and before the [[Season of the Harvest]] (''{{lang|egy|}}''). In the {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic|1=Coptic calendar}} and {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian|1=Egyptian calendar}} calendars this season begins at the start of the month of {{ill|Tobi (month)|en|Tobi (month)|WD=Q290480|lt=Tobi|1=Tobi (month)}} (about 9 January), continues through the months of {{ill|Meshir|en|Meshir|WD=Q550074|lt=Meshir|1=Meshir}} and {{ill|Paremhat|en|Paremhat|WD=Q546893|lt=Paremhat|1=Paremhat}}, before concluding at the end of {{ill|Parmouti|en|Parmouti|WD=Q2144472|lt=Parmouti|1=Parmouti}} (about 8 May).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The pronunciation of the {{ill|Egyptian language|en|Egyptian language|WD=Q50868|lt=Ancient Egyptian|1=Egyptian language}} name for the Season of the Emergence is uncertain as the hieroglyphs do not record its vowels. It is conventionally transliterated as '''Peret'''{{citation |first=Nigel C. |last=Strudwick |title=Texts from the Pyramid Age |date=2005 |page=103 }}. or '''Proyet'''. The name refers to the emergence of the fertile land beside the {{ill|Nile|en|Nile|WD=Q3392|lt=Nile|1=Nile}} from {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|WD=Q1519552|lt=its annual flood|1=Flooding of the Nile}} and the growth of vegetation and crops over the following season. It is also known as '''Winter'''. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the [[:en:Egyptian intercalary month|intercalary month]] was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[:en:Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the {{ill|Mesori|en|Mesori|WD=Q1621298|lt=fourth month|1=Mesori}} of the [[Season of the Harvest]]. This meant that the Season of the Emergence usually lasted from January to May.{{citation |last=Silverman |first=David P. |authorlink=David P. Silverman |title=Ancient Egypt |publisher=Duncan Baird Publishers |location=London |date=1997 |page=93 }}. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Emergence" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the planting and growth of Egyptian grain. ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and {{ill|Roman Egypt|en|Roman Egypt|WD=Q202311|lt=Roman periods|1=Roman Egypt}} meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] or {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}}. ==Months== The Season of the Emergence was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Prt''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[:en:Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic calendar|1=Coptic calendar}}. {| class="wikitable" align="center" style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" | |- ! colspan="2" | {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian|1=Egyptian calendar}} ! rowspan="2" | {{ill|Coptic calendar|en|Coptic calendar|WD=Q750430|lt=Coptic|1=Coptic calendar}} |- ! {{ill|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|en|Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|WD=Q2482781|lt=Transliteration|1=Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian}} !
